title: Information Complexity and Exact Communication Bounds
speaker: Mark Braverman
abstract: In this talk we will discuss information complexity -- a measure of the amount of information Alice and Bob need to exchange to solve a problem over distributed inputs. We will present an information-theoretically optimal protocol for computing the AND of two bits distributed between Alice and Bob. We prove that the information complexity of AND is ~1.4923 bits. We use the optimal protocol and its properties to obtain tight bounds for the Disjointness problem, showing that the randomized communication complexity of Disjointness on n bits is ~0.4827n ± o(n).

proof_stages.label: Randomized communication framework and motivating disjointness problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces set disjointness as the motivating communication puzzle, then sets up randomized two-party protocols, worst-case and distributional communication complexity, private randomness, and Yao's minimax principle.

proof_stages.label: Information cost as learned mutual information
proof_stages.summary: Communication length is replaced by the mutual information each party learns about the other's input, yielding an input-distribution-dependent information cost and an information complexity defined as an infimum over correct protocols, with a zero-error limiting reduction.

proof_stages.label: Amortization and reduction to the two-bit AND
proof_stages.summary: The exact equality between information complexity and amortized communication, together with an OR-of-copies variant, reduces sharp asymptotic bounds for disjointness and set intersection to understanding the information complexity of the two-bit AND function over priors.

proof_stages.label: Functional lower-bound certificate for IC of AND
proof_stages.summary: The information complexity of AND is viewed as a functional on the four-point input-distribution simplex, and boundary conditions plus one-message recursive/Bellman inequalities are formulated as sufficient certificates for lower bounds and optimality.

proof_stages.label: Infinite-round optimal protocol for AND
proof_stages.summary: For symmetric priors the proposed optimal protocol uses private continuous random stopping times on a clock, and for asymmetric priors the infinitesimal random-walk intuition determines which party should speak until a zero is certified or the state returns to the diagonal.

proof_stages.label: Verification, finite-round loss, and the AND constant
proof_stages.summary: The protocol is certified by checking the functional inequalities, discretized to approximate the infimum, shown to lose Θ(1/r²) with r rounds, and maximized to obtain the zero-error information complexity constant for AND, about 1.4922 bits.

proof_stages.label: Exact communication consequences and remaining problems
proof_stages.summary: Plugging the computed AND information costs into compression/amortization gives exact asymptotic constants for set intersection, disjointness, and small-set variants, implies round-complexity losses, and leaves open computability, depth-two composition, and fixed-error constants.

examples.name: Private one-time-pad message RA XOR X
examples.note: Alice sends a privately masked bit RA XOR X, illustrating that a transmitted bit can carry zero mutual information about X and hence why private randomness is crucial in information cost.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the message m=RA XOR x to evaluate the information revealed about x, concluding it is zero because the private pad masks x

passes.reason: The one-time-pad message is a perfectly masking construction with zero mutual information about X, the minimum possible leakage.
Uniform XOR with a private pad is a perfectly symmetric zero-leakage/minimum-information channel, used to show private coins cannot be ignored in information accounting.
Uniform XOR masking is a collapsed zero-information/minimal-leakage channel, used to show private randomness cannot be ignored.
The uniform private XOR mask makes the message independent of X, a zero-leakage boundary case, used illustratively rather than as a formal counterexample.
A uniform private XOR mask is translation-symmetric and has zero/minimal leakage, used to show that private randomness can hide information despite communication.
Uniform private XOR padding collapses the X-to-M channel to a perfectly secret zero-information channel, refuting the intuition that an X-dependent message must reveal X.
Uniform XOR masking is a maximally private zero-information channel used to show private coins cannot be treated as public information.
the uniform private XOR mask collapses the channel from X to the transcript to zero leakage, attaining the minimum possible mutual information.
A uniform private XOR mask is a highly symmetric one-time pad achieving minimal zero mutual information, used illustratively.
the uniform XOR mask is a perfectly secret zero-leakage message used to show private randomness can hide all information
Used to show private randomness can make a transmitted XOR mask reveal zero information, a perfectly hiding symmetric boundary channel.

It is used to refute the naive idea that a sent message necessarily reveals input information; as a uniform XOR mask it is a collapsed zero-information, perfectly private and highly symmetric channel.

examples.name: Equality via hashing of long files
examples.note: For the equality function under a half-equal, half-random distribution, the hash protocol concretely shows how to compute information cost and contrasts communication length with learned information.
examples.engagement_reason: he specifies the hashing protocol and prior for equality, then computes the two mutual-information contributions, getting about 1 bit for Alice and about 50.5 bits for Bob

passes.reason: The hashing protocol for equality is a standard illustrative randomized protocol, not structurally sporadic, collapsed, optimal, or maximally regular here.
The equality/fingerprinting setup is a highly regular symmetric predicate used only to illustrate an information-cost calculation.
The equality problem/prior is highly invariant under swapping and relabeling inputs, but the hash protocol is used only as an illustrative information-cost calculation.
The hashing protocol for equality is a standard randomized fingerprinting example used to compute information cost, not a structurally special object or a refutation.
The long-file hashing protocol is an ordinary nonoptimal fingerprinting example used to compute information cost.
The one-way random-hash equality protocol is a standard illustrative, nonoptimal fingerprinting protocol for computing information cost.
The equality/fingerprinting instance is a highly invariant equality example used illustratively to compute information cost.
the hashing protocol for equality is used as a standard illustrative protocol for computing information cost, not as a structural edge case.
The hash-based equality protocol is a standard illustrative fingerprinting protocol, not deployed to refute a claim.
the equality task has the usual swap/permutation symmetry and is used as an illustrative information-cost calculation
A standard randomized hashing protocol for equality is used illustratively to compute information cost, not as a structural special case.
It is a standard randomized fingerprinting protocol for equality used as a calculation example, not as an adversarial or structurally special object.

examples.name: Singleton and diagonal priors
examples.note: Degenerate input distributions, such as a singleton prior or a diagonal prior with X determined by Y, illustrate that information cost is meaningless without a specified prior and can be zero despite communication.
examples.engagement_reason: he evaluates these priors under the information-cost definition, saying singleton and diagonal priors give zero information cost since the inputs are already determined

passes.reason: Singleton and diagonal priors collapse conditional uncertainty so that inputs are already determined, giving the boundary case of zero information cost.
Point-mass and diagonal priors collapse conditional uncertainty and lie on the boundary of the prior simplex, illustrating prior dependence.
Singleton and diagonal priors are zero-conditional-entropy boundary cases of the distribution simplex, used to illustrate dependence on the prior.
Singleton or deterministic-diagonal priors have collapsed conditional uncertainty and zero information cost for any protocol, showing that prior-free information cost cannot work.
Singleton and diagonal priors are collapsed boundary distributions with zero conditional uncertainty, used to show that information cost is meaningless without a prior.
Singleton and diagonal supports are collapsed priors with zero conditional uncertainty, making every transcript have zero information and obstructing a prior-free notion.
Point-mass or deterministic diagonal priors collapse conditional uncertainty to zero, showing prior-free information cost would fail.
singleton or diagonal priors collapse the conditional uncertainty and show that a prior-free information measure would be vacuous.
Singleton and diagonal priors collapse conditional uncertainty to zero and sit on the boundary of the distribution simplex.
singleton and diagonal priors have collapsed conditional uncertainty and zero information cost, showing priors cannot be ignored
Singleton or diagonal priors collapse conditional uncertainty to zero, showing that prior-free/nontrivial information cost cannot be assumed.
These priors collapse conditional uncertainty to zero and serve as boundary cases showing that a distribution-free information-cost notion would fail.

examples.name: Asymmetric AND prior with probabilities 0.01, 0.01, 0.48, 0.4
examples.note: The concrete asymmetric distribution makes it visible that Alice should speak first, leading to the rule that the party more likely to hold a zero drives the next infinitesimal step.
examples.engagement_reason: he plugs in the asymmetric AND probabilities and reasons through which party should speak, the random-walk update, and the stopping/diagonal conditions

passes.reason: The skewed AND prior is an ordinary nondegenerate distribution used to illustrate which party should speak first.
This is an ordinary full-support but skewed AND prior used to illustrate which party should speak next.
This is just a full-support skew AND prior used to illustrate which party should speak.

This is a full-support but skewed AND input distribution used only to illustrate which party should speak.
It is an ordinary asymmetric two-bit AND prior used to illustrate which party should speak, not to disprove a claim.
It is an ordinary skewed AND prior, up to an apparent normalization slip, used only to illustrate which party should speak.
the positive asymmetric distribution on the four AND inputs is an interior illustrative prior used to decide which party should speak.
The specified AND prior is an ordinary positive asymmetric distribution used to illustrate which party should speak.
the full-support skewed AND distribution is an ordinary asymmetric prior used to illustrate which party should speak
The skewed AND prior is an ordinary asymmetric positive distribution used to illustrate which party should speak.
